result:
There is a profound, unsettling comfort in the concept of deep time. We spend our lives measuring existence in frantic, fleeting increments—the next deadline, the passing weekend, the shifting of seasons. Yet, beneath our noisy, hurried lives, the Earth moves at a pace so glacial it borders on the imperceptible. 

Mountains rise and erode over countless epochs. Continents drift across the molten mantle like lazy clouds in a summer sky. The ground we walk on is not static; it is a viscous, breathing thing, constantly recycling itself through the slow churn of tectonic plates. Every pebble on a riverbed holds a memory older than human language, older than human thought, older than the very concept of thought itself.

Sometimes, when I hold a piece of basalt or a fossilized shell, I try to reconcile its timeline with my own. This rock was here long before the first fish dared to crawl onto land. It will be here long after our cities have crumbled into dust and the oceans have boiled away. We are but a blip, a sudden spark in a long, dark corridor of millennia.

Rather than feeling diminished by this, I find it strangely liberating. Our mistakes, our heartbreaks, our grandest achievements—none of it will register on the geological clock. It grants us the permission to simply be. If the Earth will forget us in a million years, then the only thing that truly matters is the quiet, urgent beauty of the present moment.
